options.a: A class template that has been instantiated for one specific type.
options.b: A `using` declaration that takes its own template parameter(s) and expands to a different template specialization.
options.c: A way to create a new type with the same shape as another but distinct in the type system.
options.d: A forward declaration of a template before it is defined.

explanation_md: An alias template is `template <...> using Name = SomeTemplate<...>;`. It introduces a parameterised synonym — `Name<X>` expands to a specific instantiation of the underlying template. It is not a new type, not an instantiation by itself, and not a forward declaration.

`Same<int>` expands to `Pair<int, int>`, an aggregate with two `int` members. `p.first` is 5 and `p.second` is 7, and their product is 35. The alias does not change the underlying behaviour — it is purely a different way to spell the same type.

prompt_md: Given `template <typename T> using SameTypePair = Pair<T, T>;`, what is the relationship between `SameTypePair<int>` and `Pair<int, int>`?

options.a: They are different types that share a common base class.
options.b: They are the same type — the alias is a synonym, not a new type.
options.c: `SameTypePair<int>` is implicitly convertible to `Pair<int, int>` but they are distinct.
options.d: `SameTypePair<int>` is a derived type of `Pair<int, int>`.

explanation_md: Type aliases — including alias templates — do not introduce new types. They are alternative names. Any function or variable declared with one spelling matches the other exactly, which is why declaring overloads using both spellings produces a redefinition error.

options.a: Nothing — these are two valid overloads of `f`.
options.b: `SameTypePair<int>` and `Pair<int, int>` name the same type, so the second `f` is a redefinition of the first.
options.c: Alias templates cannot be used as function parameter types.
options.d: The `using` line must appear before the `Pair` template definition.

explanation_md: Alias templates produce synonyms, not new types. `SameTypePair<int>` expands to `Pair<int, int>`, so the two `f` signatures are identical. The compiler reports the second as a redefinition of the first. Order is fine in this snippet — the alias references `Pair` after it is defined.
